does: This paper analyzes how neural reward models learn features in the context of KL-regularized policy optimization, where the learned reward function is exponentiated to define the deployed policy. The authors study a two-stage neural architecture with a Gaussian single-index model where a hidden direction is first recovered from reward-weighted samples via feature learning, then a readout layer is fitted via weighted ridge regression. They establish that exponential reward weighting alters the Hermite signal structure available to the first layer, and show that above a dimension-free threshold temperature, a constant fraction of neurons successfully recover the hidden direction. The analysis yields tilted-policy value-gap bounds for both idealized label-weighted and practical surrogate-weighted fitting schemes, with explicit dependence on the deployment temperature parameter.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on neural feature learning in the single-index model regime, building on prior analyses of how networks recover hidden directions under non-standard data weighting. It connects to foundational reward modeling research by incorporating the specific feedback mechanism present in policy optimization—namely, that reward errors get exponentiated in the policy and thus have downstream consequences beyond standard prediction loss. The paper responds to the gap between treating reward modeling as a pure supervised problem versus accounting for the decision-theoretic consequences of learned rewards in reinforcement learning pipelines. It also builds on recent work in implicit bias of neural networks and feature learning under distribution shift, here specialized to the exponentially-reweighted setting that arises naturally in reward learning.
enables: This analysis enables better design of reward model training procedures in reinforcement learning by quantifying how temperature hyperparameters and feature learning dynamics affect downstream policy performance. The explicit value-gap bounds provide guidance on admissible deployment strategies and suggest how to trade off between different fitted reward models. The feature-learning perspective opens paths toward understanding when reward models trained on human preferences can reliably guide policy optimization versus when they introduce systematic errors in critical regions. Future work can extend this single-index analysis to richer model classes, study multi-stage feedback loops where policy updates induce new distribution shifts, and develop algorithms that account for the tilted geometry of reward space during training rather than only at deployment time.
